skill.name: multi-agent-orchestration
skill.description: Route AI work across Claude, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, and Ollama using documented provider selection patterns.
skill.summary: Choose the right AI provider for coding, research, large context, sandboxed execution, or local privacy needs.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, environment, and heuristic patterns. Review found these are Markdown instructions and examples, not hidden executable skill code, but the skill does guide users to run provider CLIs, use API keys, and send local content to AI providers. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Documented External Command Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill tells users to run provider CLIs and local orchestration commands. This is intended guidance, not hidden execution, but it can modify files, spend credits, or run provider-side actions if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are explicit shell and CLI examples. The context shows legitimate user-invoked orchestration guidance rather than stealthy execution.

medium_findings.title: Examples Can Forward Local File Contents to AI Providers
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Several examples embed file contents into prompts using command substitution. This is useful for analysis, but cloud providers may receive private source code or documents if users run the examples unchanged.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly read files into prompts. Gemini is a cloud provider, while Ollama is local, so the risk depends on the selected provider.

medium_findings.title: API Key Environment Variable Guidance
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The skill documents environment variables for provider API keys. This is expected setup information, but users should avoid exposing these values in logs, shell history, screenshots, or delegated prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable references are real and security relevant. They are documentation examples, so they do not prove credential theft.

medium_findings.title: Auto-Approval Mode Requires Isolation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The Codex cookbook documents full auto-approval mode. The file includes a warning, but this mode can approve actions without interactive review and should be limited to sandboxed workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The auto-approval flag is explicit and the adjacent warning confirms the risky context. The risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Scanner Cryptography and Obfuscation Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Weak cryptography, pickle, and heuristic obfuscation alerts appear in Markdown examples, model names, tables, or intentionally vulnerable sample code. No executable skill script was found that performs hidden deserialization, evasion, or cryptographic downgrade.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation or examples. I found no implementation file that executes those patterns automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented External Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill tells users to run provider CLIs and local orchestration commands. This is intended guidance, not hidden execution, but it can modify files, spend credits, or run provider-side actions if followed without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are explicit shell and CLI examples. The context shows legitimate user-invoked orchestration guidance rather than stealthy execution.

dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Several examples embed file contents into prompts using command substitution. This is useful for analysis, but cloud providers may receive private source code or documents if users run the examples unchanged.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly read files into prompts. Gemini is a cloud provider, while Ollama is local, so the risk depends on the selected provider.

dangerous_patterns.title: Auto-Approval Mode Requires Isolation
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The Codex cookbook documents full auto-approval mode. The file includes a warning, but this mode can approve actions without interactive review and should be limited to sandboxed workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The auto-approval flag is explicit and the adjacent warning confirms the risky context. The risk is operational rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: Teams often waste time choosing which AI tool should handle each task. This skill provides routing guidance, provider cookbooks, and fallback patterns for Claude, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, and Ollama.
actual_capabilities: Maps task types to suitable providers using a decision tree.
Provides cookbook steps for Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, and Ollama.
Documents fallback strategies when a provider is unavailable or rate limited.
Explains parallel agent launch patterns and result collection methods.
Summarizes authentication options and model tier selection guidance.
Highlights when work should stay in Claude instead of being delegated.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not include executable orchestration scripts.
Provider names, model names, prices, and limits may become stale.
Cloud delegation can expose prompts or file contents to third-party providers.
Some commands require local CLIs, subscriptions, API keys, or Ollama models.

use_cases.title: Route development tasks
use_cases.description: Select Claude, Codex, Cursor, or OpenCode based on context, sandboxing, and implementation needs.

use_cases.title: Plan research delegation
use_cases.description: Use Gemini or parallel agents for large context, current documentation, and cross-source synthesis.

use_cases.title: Protect sensitive work
use_cases.description: Identify when tasks should remain local with Ollama or stay in the current conversation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a provider
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multi-agent-orchestration skill to choose the best provider for this task: [describe task]. Explain the routing decision and any risks.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a delegation plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a delegation plan for [task]. Include provider choice, required credentials, context to send, expected output, and fallback provider.

prompt_templates.title: Design a parallel workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the parallel execution guidance to split [research goal] across multiple agents. Define agent roles, timeouts, synthesis steps, and confidence checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a delegation strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this delegation strategy for security, cost, privacy, and reliability risks: [paste plan]. Recommend safer routing and required user consent points.

output_examples.input: Choose a provider for testing an untrusted Python script.
output_examples.output: Recommended provider: Codex with sandboxing. Rationale: the task needs isolated execution. Do not use full auto outside a controlled workspace.

output_examples.input: Plan analysis of a large document and a screenshot.
output_examples.output: Recommended provider: Gemini. Rationale: the task needs large context and multimodal input. Confirm that the files can be sent to Google.

output_examples.input: Handle sensitive source code without cloud upload.
output_examples.output: Recommended provider: Ollama. Rationale: local inference keeps data on the machine. Check model quality before relying on the result.

best_practices: Ask for user consent before sending private files or prompts to external providers.
Check provider availability, authentication, and cost before large delegations.
Keep tasks in the current assistant when conversation history is required.
anti_patterns: Do not paste secrets, credentials, or private customer data into cloud providers without approval.
Do not use auto-approval modes for untrusted tasks outside an isolated workspace.
Do not hardcode model names when the skill recommends discovery first.

faq.question: Does this skill run agents automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions and examples for choosing and launching providers.

faq.question: Which tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Claude, Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor Agent, OpenCode CLI, and Ollama.

faq.question: Can it process sensitive data safely?
faq.answer: It recommends Ollama for local processing, but users must still review prompts and files before delegation.

faq.question: Does it require API keys?
faq.answer: Some provider workflows require API keys or subscriptions. The skill documents those requirements.

faq.question: Can it reduce AI tool costs?
faq.answer: Yes. It suggests lighter models for simple tasks and stronger models for complex synthesis.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill documents shell commands, provider CLIs, API keys, and examples that can send file contents to AI services.
